title: Lattice-Trapped Atom Interferometry with a Bose-Einstein condensate: Observation and Control of Interactions
authors: Emmett Hough, Tahiyat Rahman, Forest Tschirhart, Subhadeep Gupta
abstract: Precision interferometry with atomic wavepackets confined in a one-dimensional optical lattice is an emergent paradigm in quantum sensing of forces and fields, with applications in gravimetry, accelerometry, geophysics, and fundamental physics tests. We report on the realization of a lattice-trapped interferometer where the two arms are sourced from a weakly-interacting ytterbium Bose-Einstein condensate, coherently split and trapped by pulsed optical standing waves before recombination. We directly observe atomic interactions through contrast changes and phase shifts of the interferometer. By changing either the atom number or the sample volume to vary the density, we demonstrate control over interactions and optimize interferometer performance. Our observations are effectively captured by a mean-field theoretical model of the system. This work experimentally probes the boundary where improved performance from source brightening through higher phase space density transitions into a regime beyond single-atom physics in lattice-trapped atom interferometry, and opens a door to incorporating many-body effects for metrological advances in such platforms.
summary: This paper demonstrates an atom interferometer using a Ytterbium Bose-Einstein Condensate trapped in an optical lattice. The authors show that interatomic interactions can be controlled by tuning the density of the BEC, and that using a BEC source can significantly reduce statistical phase error compared to thermal sources.
main_problem: Investigating how mean-field interactions and density-dependent effects impact the coherence, phase, and performance of a lattice-trapped atom interferometer using a BEC source.
main_result: The researchers demonstrated direct observation and control of atomic interactions by tuning BEC density, showing that a BEC source can outperform a thermal source in statistical phase error and atom flux.
method: The experiment uses a 1D optical lattice-trapped interferometer with a Ytterbium BEC, employing Bragg pulses for splitting and Bloch oscillations for path separation, while varying expansion time and atom number to control density.
model_or_system: A 1D optical lattice-trapped interferometer using a 174-Yb Bose-Einstein Condensate. The system is modeled using the Gross-Pitaevskii equation and a mean-field model for density-induced phase shifts.
key_observables: Interferometer contrast (C), global phase shift (delta-phi), atom number asymmetry (A), and statistical phase error.
important_parameters: Lattice depth (4-5 Erecc), peak density (approx 2.5e14 cm^-3), s-wave scattering length (5.6 nm), and Bloch oscillation period (836 us).
main_assumptions: The model assumes a self-similar ballistic expansion of the BEC and neglects mean-field effects on the velocity distribution after the velocity selection pulse.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the interferometer sequence; Fig 2 shows contrast decay vs hold time and separation; Fig 3 shows contrast vs expansion time and atom number; Fig 5 compares BEC and thermal source performance; Fig 6 shows a single fringe at long hold time.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the lattice-trapped paradigm, describes the experimental setup and BEC preparation, details the interaction control methods, presents results on contrast and phase shifts, compares BEC vs thermal sources, and discusses decoherence and limitations.
why_interesting: It explores the transition from single-atom physics to many-body dynamics in a sensing platform, providing insights into how mean-field effects can be harnessed or mitigated for improved quantum metrology.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Space-time diagram of the interferometer se- quence. The BEC expands before velocity-selection with a first-order Bragg pulse (vertical dashed line “Clean Up”) and launch via Bloch oscillations (striped curves). Two π/2 pulses create the superposition loaded into the lattice trap at the kinematic apex. A symmetric pair of π/2 pulses close the in- terferometer and another BO sequence (“Push”) spatially sep- arates the output ports. (b) Representative absorption image at the interferometer output with all six distinct paths re- solved (here ∆z = 120d). Representative interference fringes observed in the asymmetry, obtained by (c) varying the phase of the final π/2 pulse and by (d)...

FIG. 2. Contrast decay of the lattice-trapped interferometer with hold time for ∆z = 15d for BEC sources at two dif- ferent expansion times. Dashed lines are exponential fits, with 1/e decay times τC/TBO,g = 189(26), 941(113) for texp = 8.4 ms, 16.0 ms respectively. Inset: contrast decay with wavepacket separation for a BEC source at τhold = 200 TBO,g and texp = 16.0 ms. Solid line is an exponential fit with 1/e decay length κ/d = 70(8). Error bars represent 1σ fit uncer- tainties.

FIG. 3. Control of the contrast at τhold/TBO,g = 200 and ∆z/d = 15 by varying the density of the BEC source. (a) Observed contrast for various texp. Inset: fringes at texp = 6.8 ms (diamonds) and texp = 14.5 ms (squares). (b) Observed contrast at texp = 8.4 ms for various atom numbers. All error bars represent the standard error on the mean. Shaded bands in (a) and (b) show the theoretical results from a mean-field model incorporating typical 5% number fluctuations (see text).

FIG. 4. Global phase shift due to mean-field interactions ob- served by biasing the interferometer arms with an imbalance IN of 0.1 (squares) and 0.7 (circles), for texp = 8.4 ms. Er- ror bars represent the standard error on the mean. Shaded bands show the theoretical results from a mean-field model in- corporating typical 5% number fluctuations (see text). Inset: absorption image of atoms after the first π/2 pulse showing a number imbalance of 0.7.

FIG. 5. Comparison of BEC (texp = 16 ms) fringes with those from a thermal source (initially at 350 nK, texp = 8.4 ms). (a) Fringes at three hold times for ∆z = 15d for thermal (red diamonds) and BEC (blue circles) sources. (b) Total atom number as a function of hold time. (c) Statistical phase error from sinusoidal fits. The thermal data has an average of 5 points per phase scanned, whereas the BEC has 4.
